content:
First, note that for a positive integer \(n\) with digit‐product \(P(n)\), we seek

  \(P(n)=n^2-15n-27.\)

Since \(P(n)\ge0\), the right‐hand side must be nonnegative, i.e.  
\[n^2-15n-27\ge0\;\Longrightarrow\;n\ge\frac{15+\sqrt{225+108}}2\approx16.62,\]
so \(n\ge17\).

On the other hand, if \(n\) has \(d\) digits then \(P(n)\le9^d\), while for large \(n\), \(n^2-15n-27\) grows much faster than \(9^d\).  One checks:

- For 1‐digit \(n\le9\), the RHS is negative.
- For 2‐digit \(n\ge10\), the maximum digit‐product is \(9\cdot9=81\).  Requiring
  \[
    n^2-15n-27\le81
    \quad\Longrightarrow\quad
    n^2-15n-108\le0
    \quad\Longrightarrow\quad
    n\le20.3,
  \]
  so the only candidates in the allowed range \(n\ge17\) are \(17,18,19,20\).

Compute each:

- \(n=17:\;P(17)=1\cdot7=7,\;17^2-15\cdot17-27=289-255-27=7\). ✓  
- \(n=18:\;P(18)=8,\;18^2-270-27=27\).  No.  
- \(n=19:\;P(19)=9,\;361-285-27=49\).  No.  
- \(n=20:\;P(20)=0,\;400-300-27=73\).  No.  

Hence the only solution is \(n=17\), and the sum of all such \(n\) is

\[
\boxed{17}.
\]

  \(P(n)=n^2-15n-27.\)

- For 1‐digit \(n\le9\), the RHS is negative.
- For 2‐digit \(n\ge10\), the maximum digit‐product is \(9\cdot9=81\).  Requiring
  \[
    n^2-15n-27\le81
    \quad\Longrightarrow\quad
    n^2-15n-108\le0
    \quad\Longrightarrow\quad

- \(n=20:\;P(20)=0,\;400-300-27=73\).  No.
